title: Is Our Movement Not a Treasury of Trust!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored from the printed page, and clear OCR numeral, word-break, spacing, and punctuation errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam has announced a protest campaign at 10 a.m. on 3 November in every district headquarters, demanding the withdrawal of the milk-price and electricity-tariff increases and the immediate announcement of relief for farmers whose crops across lakhs of acres have been ruined by the heavy rain.

The AIADMK Government has raised the price of milk by Rs 10 a litre. No Government has ever imposed an increase of Rs 10 at one stroke. This is the second increase in the three and a half years since Jayalalithaa came to power. Immediately after taking office in 2011, they raised the DMK-era price of Rs 17.75 a litre to Rs 24, an increase of Rs 6.25. They have now added another Rs 10 and made it Rs 34. If present Chief Minister Panneerselvam is questioned, he may say: “Our Amma raised it by Rs 6 a litre. Only by raising it by another Rs 10 can I show that I follow Amma's path. That is why I have done so!”

Panneerselvam's statement says that when the procurement price was raised by Rs 3 a litre from 1 January 2014, the selling price was not changed. Why was it not changed then? Was it not because poor and ordinary people would suffer? Why, after now raising the procurement price by Rs 5 for cow's milk and Rs 4 for buffalo milk, have you raised the selling price by Rs 10? Will poor and middle-class families not suffer? Has the concern shown for them in January vanished?

You have doubled the procurement-price increase while fixing the retail increase. Do you imagine that families, children and the elderly will escape the impact? Even a very poor household buying one litre in the morning and one in the evening must now spend Rs 600 more every month. When purchasing power is steadily shrinking, can people afford this extra expense?

The announcement says the procurement-price increase was decided by “the Tamil Nadu Government functioning in the path of Heart's Deity, Revolutionary Leader Amma.” But the retail-price announcement does not say it was done in Amma's path. Are they going to claim that announcements which do not hurt people belong to “People's Chief Minister Amma,” while those which hurt them belong to Panneerselvam?

Panneerselvam also alleges that Aavin was driven into the abyss under the DMK Government. When procurement prices were increased on 1 September 2009 and 5 January 2011 to benefit milk producers, the DMK Government did not raise the retail price of Rs 17.75 by even one paisa, because poor and middle-class consumers should not be hurt. In just three and a half years, the AIADMK has raised it twice, by a total of Rs 16.25, fixing it at Rs 34. This comparison reveals both the quality of DMK rule and the anti-people character of AIADMK rule. Is keeping the retail price unchanged what Panneerselvam calls driving Aavin into the abyss?

If Aavin fell so low under the DMK, why did Jayalalithaa remove Dairy Development Minister Madhavaram Moorthy in her last cabinet reshuffle? Was it because he had lifted Aavin out of the abyss? Who was Vaidyanathan, arrested for large-scale adulteration of Aavin milk? Was he not an AIADMK functionary? Why was he arrested? How did a former daily-wage worker become a wealthy owner of eighty-three vehicles? Why were three Aavin employees suspended last month? Why did Jayalalithaa expel contractor Vaidyanathan from the AIADMK? How many crores did Aavin lose, and who must accept responsibility, the DMK Government or the AIADMK Government? Is it clear now who drove Aavin into the abyss? Carrying this weight of corruption on its back, does this regime possess any standing to blame the DMK?

Even before announcing the milk-price increase, the Government planned an electricity-tariff rise. The Electricity Board and the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission published the proposal and held a consultation with the public, consumers, engineers and other groups. Every section represented there strongly opposed the increase.

The Regulatory Commission proposed an increase exceeding fifteen per cent. Those opposing it included representatives of the public; S. Gandhi, president of the Tamil Nadu Electricity Engineers Association; T. Gopalakrishnan, vice-president of the All India BSNL Pensioners Welfare Association; P. V. Chandrasekar, Southern Railway's chief electrical engineer; Ann Josey, a researcher with Prayas Energy Group; and representatives of several associations.

S. Gandhi pointed out that the Electricity Board's revenue report usually runs to more than 450 pages, whereas this year's report did not even contain twenty-five. How could anyone fully understand the Board's functioning from such a truncated document?

Commission member Nagalsamy said the Board was legally required to submit annual income and expenditure accounts but, for some reason, did not do so. Even without seeking a tariff increase, it should at least disclose expenditure and revenue. The Board supplied some information, but the Commission had no opportunity to test its truth or examine accurate details. The proposed increase was therefore announced on assumptions based on the limited information available. The public should have received all necessary details to present informed views, but the Board withheld them.

Nagalsamy added that he did not support the figures published on those assumptions. The Commission had ordered the Board not to buy power from unapproved sources for more than Rs 3.50 a unit, but the order was ignored. It also ordered the Board not to purchase power at the excessive rate of Rs 12 to Rs 14 a unit, but that too was ignored. Even the direction not to purchase power without Commission approval was not implemented. The AIADMK Government must answer these serious allegations of financial misuse openly. Does the minister for the department exist, or is he occupied day and night with his more “important duty”?

When the DMK Government made a very small tariff increase, the AIADMK leader issued a statement condemning it. Can she now remain silent while her own Government raises tariffs? Was the increase imposed within six months of taking office not enough? Must there be another?

I have already explained the proposed increase, designed to bring the Electricity Board Rs 6,805 crore in revenue by raising tariffs between fifteen and seventeen per cent. For domestic consumers using 201 to 500 units in two months, the rate is proposed to rise from Rs 4 to Rs 4.60 a unit. Above 500 units, it will rise from Rs 5.75 to Rs 6.60. The State subsidy applies only up to 500 units. In today's living conditions, an ordinary middle-class household may use at least 600 units in two months. A household now paying Rs 3,450 for 600 units will have to add Rs 510 and pay Rs 3,960.

The people of Tamil Nadu know well that whenever the AIADMK comes to power it habitually squeezes them through one increase after another: electricity, bus fares and milk. Jayalalithaa raised electricity tariffs from 1 December 2001 after becoming Chief Minister that year. In 2003, while she remained Chief Minister, she imposed increases worth Rs 1,398 crore from 15 March. Ignoring opposition from every section and continually raising milk prices, electricity tariffs and bus fares has become the defining practice of AIADMK rule.

Yesterday I described how ten days of heavy rain have affected every section of Tamil Nadu. Farmers in particular have been struck again where they were already wounded. Crops cultivated with borrowed money for inputs lie submerged. Though the rain continued, no minister visited the flooded areas. No one wiped away the farmers' tears or came forward to console them.

Keeping all this in mind, I announced that the DMK will hold its protest campaign on the morning of 3 November in every district headquarters under the leadership of the district secretaries. Our treasurer, Thalapathi M. K. Stalin, has contacted every district secretary and asked them to begin preparations immediately, conduct the protest impressively, and exert the necessary pressure on Tamil Nadu's rulers.
You must not think, “Only the treasurer asked us. The general secretary and the leader did not. The leader merely issued an announcement,” must you? That is why I am writing this letter.

On seeing our protest announcement and recognising its justice, the AIADMK Government may itself reduce the milk price, declare that it will not raise electricity tariffs, and announce relief for farmers. Let us wait and see! That is why we have left a week's interval before the protest.
Whether they make such an announcement or not, I trust that you will begin preparations for the protest in full force today itself. Is the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam not that treasury of trust!
With affection,
M.K.
